hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: You kicked off the action with a raise, and your opponent called, setting the stage for a potentially explosive hand. The stakes were high, and the tension was palpable.
Flop — Ace, 5, 6. You bet, trying to assert your dominance, but your opponent called, refusing to back down.
Turn — King. You checked, giving your opponent the opportunity to take control, and they seized it, betting big, a sudden and unexpected move that put you on notice.
River — 5. Your opponent fired again for value, determined to maximize their winnings and leave no doubt about their hand's strength.

wrongReasons: J 6 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with J 6.
Q 5 makes a three of a kind here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
10 4 doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only one pair wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 7 K

Your opponent's hand improved significantly on the Turn when they made One Pair with the King, and then again on the River when they made Two Pair with the 5. The 7 K hand fits perfectly with the betting pattern, as the opponent's sudden aggression on the Turn and continued pressure on the River suggest a strong hand that improved at the right moments.
